commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: See how much close investigation is made by the many in a long course of time, that it should not be said that the trial was hurried over. For, as the orator had made mention of Lysias, that he took him away with violence, Felix, he says, deferred them. Having knowledge of that way: that is, he put them off on purpose: not because he wanted to learn, but as wishing to get rid of the Jews. On their account, he did not like to let him go: to punish him was not possible; that would have been (too) barefaced. And to let him have liberty, and to forbid none of his acquaintance to minister to him. So entirely did he too acquit him of the charges. Howbeit, to gratify them, he detained him, and besides, expecting to receive money, he called for Paul. And after certain days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ. And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance (i. self-control or chastity), and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go your way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for you. He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him; wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him. But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix's room: and Felix, willing to show the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound.
commentaries.content_en: See how much close investigation is made by the many in a long course of time, that it should not be said that the trial was hurried over. For, as the orator had made mention of Lysias, that he took him away with violence, Felix, he says, deferred them. Having knowledge of that way: that is, he put them off on purpose: not because he wanted to learn, but as wishing to get rid of the Jews. On their account, he did not like to let him go: to punish him was not possible; that would have been (too) barefaced. And to let him have liberty, and to forbid none of his acquaintance to minister to him. So entirely did he too acquit him of the charges. Howbeit, to gratify them, he detained him, and besides, expecting to receive money, he called for Paul. And after certain days, when Felix came with his wife Drusilla, which was a Jewess, he sent for Paul, and heard him concerning the faith in Christ. And as he reasoned of righteousness, temperance (i. self-control or chastity), and judgment to come, Felix trembled, and answered, Go your way for this time; when I have a convenient season, I will call for you. He hoped also that money should have been given him of Paul, that he might loose him; wherefore he sent for him the oftener, and communed with him. But after two years Porcius Festus came into Felix's room: and Felix, willing to show the Jews a pleasure, left Paul bound.
commentaries.content_pt: Veja quão minuciosa é a investigação realizada por muitos ao longo de um longo período de tempo, para que não se diga que o julgamento foi apressado. Pois, como o orador mencionou Lysias, que o levou à força, Félix, diz ele, os adiou. Tendo conhecimento daquele caminho: isto é, ele os adiou de propósito: não porque quisesse aprender, mas por desejar se livrar dos judeus. Por causa deles, ele não gostava de deixá-lo ir: puni-lo não era possível; isso seria (demais) descarado. E permitir que ele tivesse liberdade, e não proibir nenhum de seus conhecidos de ministrar a ele. Assim, ele também o isentou completamente das acusações. No entanto, para agradá-los, ele o deteve e, além disso, esperando receber dinheiro, chamou Paulo. E depois de alguns dias, quando Félix veio com sua esposa Drusila, que era judia, mandou chamar Paulo e ouviu-o a respeito da fé em Cristo. E enquanto ele argumentava sobre justiça, temperança (ou seja, autocontrole ou castidade) e o juízo vindouro, Félix tremeu e respondeu: Vai-te por agora; quando tiver uma ocasião conveniente, te chamarei. Ele também esperava que dinheiro lhe fosse dado por Paulo, para que pudesse soltá-lo; por isso, o chamava com mais frequência e conversava com ele. Mas, após dois anos, Pórcio Festo assumiu o lugar de Félix: e Félix, desejando agradar aos judeus, deixou Paulo preso.

ai_summary.one_sentence: João Crisóstomo analisa a prisão de Paulo e a manipulação política de Félix.
ai_summary.abstract: Neste comentário, João Crisóstomo examina a situação de Paulo diante de Félix, destacando a astúcia política do governador romano. Ele observa que Félix adiou o julgamento de Paulo não por interesse em justiça, mas para agradar aos judeus e, possivelmente, obter subornos. A interação entre Paulo e Félix revela a tensão entre a fé cristã e a política, com Félix mostrando medo ao ouvir sobre justiça e juízo vindouro. Crisóstomo enfatiza a injustiça da detenção de Paulo e a manipulação de Félix, que, mesmo sabendo da inocência de Paulo, optou por mantê-lo preso para evitar conflitos com os judeus. Essa análise reflete a complexidade das relações entre poder e fé na época.
key_points: Félix adiou o julgamento de Paulo por motivos políticos.
A interação entre Paulo e Félix revela a tensão entre fé e política.
Crisóstomo critica a injustiça da detenção de Paulo.
Félix esperava um suborno para libertar Paulo.
A resposta de Félix ao discurso de Paulo demonstra sua inquietação moral.

argumentative_structure.thesis: A análise de Crisóstomo revela a manipulação política de Félix em relação a Paulo.
arguments: Félix não tinha interesse genuíno em justiça, mas em agradar aos judeus.
A detenção de Paulo reflete a corrupção e a injustiça do sistema judicial romano.
argumentative_structure.conclusion: A situação de Paulo ilustra como a fé pode ser ameaçada por interesses políticos e corrupção.

spiritual_insight.theme: A fé em Cristo é testada em meio à injustiça.
spiritual_insight.practical_reflection: Devemos permanecer firmes em nossa fé, mesmo quando enfrentamos injustiças e manipulações.
